chunk_summary: National Deworming Day ensures safe albendazole dosing for children aged 1–19 years, with mop-up rounds for absentees. Safety protocols emphasize exclusion of acutely unwell children and preparedness for minor adverse effects.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific albendazole dosing guidelines for National Deworming Day.
Learn safety protocols for deworming implementation in schools and anganwadis.
Recognize the role of deworming in anemia prevention and pediatric growth.
Identify strategies for managing minor adverse effects during deworming rounds.
key_facts: Albendazole dosing: 200 mg for children 1–2 years, 400 mg for children 2–19 years.
Routine dosing is not recommended for children below 1 year.
Mop-up rounds target children absent or not dosed during the main round.
Safety exclusion criteria: fever, severe abdominal pain, repeated vomiting, or unsafe oral administration.
Minor adverse effects include transient nausea, abdominal discomfort, and vomiting.
Periodic deworming complements dietary changes and iron-folic acid (IFA) supplementation.
reference_quote: Albendazole dosing in national guidance is age-based: 200 mg for children 1–2 years (half of a 400 mg chewable tablet) and 400 mg for children 2–19 years (one full tablet).
document_key_topics: Role of deworming in anemia prevention
